original_abstract:
The Epistle of James presents a significant historical-critical enigma, characterized by a striking lack of explicit Christological content and a distinct lack of focus on the life, death, or resurrection of Jesus. While many modern scholars categorize the work as a general Christian moral exhortation or a reaction to Pauline theology, this article revives and expands upon an older hypothesis: that the document’s Jewish orientation and "Christian silences" are the result of a deliberate authorial strategy. By analyzing the literal address to the "twelve tribes in the Dispersion," the absence of typical Christian greetings, and the use of the synagogue as a primary setting, I argue that the epistle was crafted by a Jewish Christian seeking to maintain an irenic presence within the broader Jewish community. 

Drawing on varied ancient parallels—including the works of Lactantius, Theophilus of Antioch, and the Qumran text 4QMMT—I demonstrate that early religious writers frequently employed strategic reticence for apologetic or social purposes. This article suggests that James functions as a bridge, utilizing common ethical and wisdom traditions to foster tolerance and provide a "Christian version of Judaism" that could be understood and respected by non-Christian Jews. Ultimately, the epistle is viewed not as a theological vacuum, but as a sophisticated presentation designed to show that followers of Jesus remained faithful members of the synagogue, thereby mitigating communal hostility and articulating a shared moral identity.
humanized_abstract:
The Epistle of James is probably the most puzzling text in the New Testament from a historical-critical perspective. It stands alone within the New Testament in that it does not exhibit any Christology and lacks any direct references to the life, death, or resurrection of Jesus. Explanations abound in the contemporary period, particularly in regard to whether it is seen as a general NT Christian ethical treatise or a polemic against Paul or the Pauline message. However, there has been a view, in some form, at least from ancient times which is worthy of revitalisation, especially in light of more recent scholarship. Viewed in the context of the entire letter, its distinctly Jewish flavour together with what has been termed variously, “Christian silences,” can be better explained as the intended outcome of the author’s theological strategy, as will be argued by an examination of: 1. The literal address to the twelve tribes of the Dispersion; 2. The omission of the customary Christian greeting; 3. The general and almost exclusive use of the synagogue in James.

Drawing on a wide range of ancient parallels as diverse as those of Lactantius, Theophilus of Antioch, and 4QMMT from the Qumran library, I argue that early authors sometimes invoked silence for rhetorical and sociological reasons. With James drawing on more commonplace ethical and wisdom traditions in order to foster Jewish Christians' acceptance in their broader synagogal context in a manner that could be understood as a sort of Christian version of Judaism, the Letter of James cannot be viewed as a theological zero-sum game where every affirmation requires an opposing denial, but as an articulated whole which, in its ability to show Jewish Christians that they were still full members of the synagogal community, served to mollify that very community while at the same time expressing a moral solidarity in a manner that reinforced the Christian identity of the Jamesan community.
